Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to carve out time on weekends mostly, but with limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could access good internet or find a quiet space. I'm a visual learner, so I'd focus heavily on watching lecture videos and creating diagrams, but fitting in those six hours a week felt like a struggle sometimes. I'd batch things together when I could rather than spreading them evenly.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I wish I could say I had a set schedule, but life kept getting in the way—work, family stuff, just feeling drained. When deadlines approached, I'd suddenly find the energy to push through, but regular weekly studying wasn't really my thing. The stress of cramming wasn't great for my mental state, but somehow I made it work.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed, especially since my attendance wasn't the best. The fear of falling behind was a big motivator, even when I didn't feel like engaging. Occasionally peer support messages would prompt me to check in too.
Q4: There were a few weeks mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. I think it happened when I found a rhythm with the video lectures—I'd watch them while eating dinner, and it just became part of my evening. But that routine broke down when things got overwhelming with other responsibilities. It never fully stuck as a permanent habit.
Q5: The video content was absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I'd rewatch certain sections multiple times to really understand concepts, which is probably why my watch time was so high. The discussion forums were also helpful when I actually engaged—seeing how others interpreted things gave me different perspectives. Anything with clear visual explanations or diagrams worked best for my brain.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the reading materials, especially dense PDFs without visuals. They just didn't stick for me the way videos did, and with limited time, I had to prioritize. Some of the optional supplementary resources got ignored completely because I was barely keeping up with required stuff. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to my grades or understanding of key concepts. Visual content that broke down complex ideas made me feel like I was actually learning something. I also valued things that didn't take forever—efficiency mattered a lot when I only had about six hours a week to dedicate to studying. If something felt like busywork, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required work had this weight to it that forced me to engage even when I was exhausted or sad. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got deprioritized because I was just trying to survive the essentials. There was guilt about skipping them, but not enough to change my behavior.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then studied them offline later. I'd create my own visual notes and diagrams on paper, which helped me process information better than just staring at a screen. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed materials spread across my bed, completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q10: Most of my deep studying happened offline after I'd downloaded what I needed. I'd pause videos, sketch out concepts, and really work through problems on paper for hours. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Also, when I was preparing for exams, I was reviewing my handwritten notes constantly—that's probably why I scored 100 on the exam despite moderate LMS activity.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them offline for days. My internet access isn't always reliable, and honestly, I focus better without the distraction of being online. One download could fuel a whole week of studying that the system would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when I was feeling really sad and my motivation just tanked. Around midterms, everything piled up at once and I felt paralyzed. The peer support intervention helped pull me back a bit, but there were weeks where logging in felt impossible. Competing responsibilities and just emotional exhaustion made engagement really hard.
Q13: All of those factors affected me at different times. Fatigue was probably the biggest one—I'd log in intending to study but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because facing it felt overwhelming. Limited resources meant I had to be strategic, which sometimes looked like disengagement from the outside.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about personal stuff or just zoning out from stress. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, just going through the motions. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was mentally engaged at all.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required energy I didn't have. Writing my thoughts out for others to see felt vulnerable when I was already feeling down. I knew participating mattered, but the emotional cost felt too high some days. Assignments I'd delay until the pressure became unbearable, then power through at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be quiet for days, then suddenly very active right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could override my low motivation and sadness. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less, honestly.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to keep going, especially when I was doubting myself. Seeing that my assignment scores were strong gave me confidence that my approach was working, even if it wasn't conventional. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd disengage while waiting. The peer support check-ins also counted as feedback in a way—knowing someone noticed helped.
Q18: The course structure with multiple online components actually worked okay for me since I could access things on my own schedule. Having eight courses meant I had to be selective about where I put my energy, and this course's flexibility helped. But the structure also made it easy to fall behind when I wasn't feeling motivated, since nothing forced daily engagement.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier for someone like me who learns visually. What made it harder was when materials weren't downloadable or required constant internet access. Also, having so many discussion requirements was tough when I was feeling sad and withdrawn—that social component felt like an extra burden rather than support sometimes.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or lost, clicking around trying to figure things out without actually completing anything. Or maybe they're struggling with the material and spending lots of time without making progress. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues who keeps trying to engage but can't follow through. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was moderate, but I scored 100 on the exam and got a distinction. The secret was all the offline work—downloading materials, creating visual notes, really processing information away from the computer. The system only saw the tip of the iceberg of my actual studying. Efficiency matters more than time logged.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which reveals something about habits. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning. They can't capture whether you're actually understanding material or just going through motions. The emotional and cognitive side of learning is invisible to any tracking system.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when my brain is actively working through concepts, making connections, asking questions even if just to myself. For me, engagement looks like creating visual maps of ideas and losing track of time while studying. It's internal and doesn't always show up in external metrics.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I was struggling emotionally while still pushing through academically. It wouldn't capture the hours spent with handwritten notes or the mental effort of studying while feeling sad. It also wouldn't show how peer support made a real difference in keeping me connected, or how my high exam score came from deep offline preparation rather than platform activity.
